title: Where Data Lives: Frontend Data-Fetching & State Architecture
description: The biggest architectural decisions in a modern frontend are about *where data lives* and *when it's fetched*. Start by separating **server state** (an async cache of data you don't own) from **client state**, then reason through normalized vs. document caches, request deduplication, optimistic updates and rollback, request waterfalls vs. parallel fetching, prefetch, `staleTime` tuning, and how Suspense reshapes component design. Aimed at the React/TanStack Query stack where these are daily decisions.

questions.title: What really separates server state from client state?
questions.description: Teams keep reaching for a library like TanStack Query or SWR for API data, but plain `useState`/Zustand/Redux for things like the open/closed sidebar.

options.text: Server state is an **asynchronous cache of data owned elsewhere** that can go stale without the client knowing â€” so it needs caching, staleness, background refetch, and dedup. Client state is owned by the app and changed synchronously, so it just needs to be stored and read.
Server state is any data that physically lives in a database; client state is any data held in JavaScript variables.
Server state must be global and client state must be local to a component â€” the split is purely about scope.
Server state is read-only and client state is writable; that's why they need different tools.

questions.explanation:
The useful mental model (popularized by TanStack Query's author and TkDodo) is that **server state is a cache of data you don't own**. You only ever hold a *copy*; the source of truth is a remote server that other clients can change at any moment, and your copy can silently become stale. That single property is what forces an entire feature set: caching, a freshness/`staleTime` policy, background revalidation, request deduplication, retries, and garbage collection.

**Client state** â€” theme, which modal is open, an unsaved form draft â€” is owned entirely by the app, changes synchronously, and has exactly one source of truth (the app itself). It needs none of that machinery, so a reducer or a `useState` is enough.

The distractors latch onto incidental traits. *Where bytes physically live* is irrelevant â€” client state can be persisted to a DB and server state can sit in memory. *Scope* is orthogonal: client state is often global (theme), and server data is often consumed locally. And server state is very much **writable** (that's what mutations are for); the point is that you write *through* it to a source you don't control.
questions.references: [TanStack Query: Does TanStack Query replace client state?](https://tanstack.com/query/latest/docs/framework/react/guides/does-this-replace-client-state)

questions.title: Which of these are client state?
questions.description: You're deciding what belongs in a server-cache library (TanStack Query) versus ordinary client state (`useState`/a store). Select **all** that apply.

options.text: The current UI theme (light / dark).
Whether a confirmation modal is currently open.
The text a user has typed into a search box but not yet submitted.
The list of orders returned by `GET /api/orders`.

questions.explanation:
Apply the ownership test: *who is the source of truth, and can it change behind your back?*

- **Theme** â€” the app owns it; no server can change it underneath you. Client state.
- **Modal open/closed** â€” pure ephemeral UI; client state.
- **Unsubmitted search text** â€” a draft that exists only in the browser until the user acts on it; client state. (Once submitted, the *results* it fetches are server state.)
- **`GET /api/orders`** â€” a copy of data owned by the backend that other clients can mutate. This is the textbook case for a server-cache library: cache it, mark it stale after `staleTime`, dedup concurrent reads, refetch in the background.

A common anti-pattern is dumping fetched API data into Redux/Zustand and then hand-writing loading flags, refetch logic, and cache invalidation â€” effectively re-implementing a query cache by hand. Keep server state in the cache library and reserve your store for genuine client state.

questions.title: With default TanStack Query, why does the roster still show the old name?
questions.description: Two queries hold the same user. The profile screen reads `['user', 7]`; a team roster reads `['team', 'roster']`, whose array embeds user 7's name. A mutation renames user 7 and writes the new name into `['user', 7]` with `setQueryData`. The profile updates instantly â€” but the roster still shows the **old** name until it refetches.

options.text: TanStack Query is a **document cache keyed by query key**, not a normalized entity cache. Each query keeps its own independent copy of user 7, so writing to `['user', 7]` leaves the copy embedded in `['team', 'roster']` untouched until that query is invalidated or refetched.
`['team', 'roster']` has too high a `staleTime`, so it ignored the update.
React's reconciler skipped the roster because the surrounding array reference didn't change.
Apollo Client would behave identically â€” no client cache propagates an entity update across separate queries.

questions.explanation:
TanStack Query deliberately stores **whole query results as opaque documents**, addressed by their query key. It does not parse your data into entities, so it has no idea that `['user', 7]` and a user object *inside* `['team', 'roster']` are 'the same' user. Updating one document can't propagate to the other.

A **normalized cache** (Apollo Client, RTK Query with an entity adapter) flattens responses into a single store keyed by entity identity (e.g. `User:7`). Every query then references that one record, so a single write updates everywhere at once. That's the precise capability the "Apollo Client would behave identically" distractor denies â€” normalized caches *do* propagate, which is exactly their selling point.

With TanStack Query you reconcile cross-query consistency yourself, usually by **invalidation**:

```ts
// after the rename mutation succeeds
queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['team', 'roster'] });
```

`staleTime` is a red herring: it controls *when a query refetches*, not whether one query's write leaks into another. And React did re-render the profile fine; the roster simply still holds old bytes.

questions.title: How many network requests does TanStack Query fire?
questions.description: Three separate components mount in the **same render** and each calls `useQuery({ queryKey: ['user', 7], queryFn: fetchUser })` with default options. The cache is empty.
questions.explanation:
Exactly **1**. TanStack Query *deduplicates by query key*: the first observer to need data starts a fetch, and any other observer that subscribes to the same key while that request is in flight attaches to the **same promise** instead of starting its own. All three components share one request and one cache entry, then re-render together when it resolves.

This in-flight sharing is why you can call `useQuery` for the same resource in many components without coordinating them â€” the cache is the coordination layer. Note the boundary: dedup is keyed, so three *different* keys would fire three requests, and a request that has already resolved isn't 'in flight' (a later mount may trigger a background refetch if the data is stale, governed by `staleTime`).

questions.title: Why cancel queries before an optimistic write?
questions.description: The canonical optimistic-update recipe calls `await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey })` at the very top of `onMutate`, *before* it snapshots and writes the optimistic value.

options.text: An already-in-flight background refetch for that key could resolve **after** your optimistic `setQueryData` and overwrite it with server data that doesn't yet include your change. Cancelling outstanding fetches stops that race.
It stops the mutation itself from being sent twice to the server.
It clears the cache entry so your optimistic value starts from a clean slate.
It is required to obtain the `context` object that `onError` later uses for rollback.

questions.explanation:
Optimistic updates are a race against the network. The danger: a `refetch` for `['todos']` was already running when the user clicked. You then `setQueryData` to the optimistic value â€” but moments later that older refetch resolves and writes its (pre-mutation) result on top, visibly reverting your optimistic change. `cancelQueries` aborts any outstanding fetches for the key so nothing clobbers your write:

```ts
onMutate: async (newTodo) => {
  await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['todos'] }); // stop in-flight refetches
  const previousTodos = queryClient.getQueryData(['todos']); // snapshot for rollback
  queryClient.setQueryData(['todos'], (old) => [...old, newTodo]); // optimistic write
  return { previousTodos }; // becomes `context` in onError / onSettled
}
```

The distractors miss the target: deduping the *mutation* isn't what this does; you don't clear the cache (you *snapshot* it so you can roll back); and the rollback `context` comes from what you **return** from `onMutate`, not from `cancelQueries`.

questions.title: Which statements correctly describe the cache-driven optimistic-update pattern in `useMutation`?

options.text: The value returned from `onMutate` becomes the `context` argument passed to `onError` and `onSettled` â€” that's where you stash the pre-mutation snapshot for rollback.
`onError` rolls back by writing the snapshot back into the cache with `setQueryData(queryKey, context.previous)`.
`onSettled` typically calls `invalidateQueries` so the cache reconciles with the server after the mutation finishes, whether it succeeded or failed.
Optimistic updates require a normalized cache and are impossible with TanStack Query's document cache.

questions.explanation:
The four callbacks form a closed loop around the cache:

- **`onMutate(variables)`** runs before the request. Cancel in-flight queries, snapshot the current data, apply the optimistic write, and **return** the snapshot â€” TanStack Query threads that return value through as `context`.
- **`onError(err, variables, context)`** fires if the mutation rejects. You restore `context.previous` with `setQueryData`, undoing the optimistic change.
- **`onSettled`** runs after success *or* error; invalidating the key triggers a refetch so the cache ends up matching the server's real result either way.

The false option inverts reality: optimistic updates are *easiest* on a document cache, because you simply write the predicted result straight into the query's data. A normalized cache is a different (and also valid) strategy, not a prerequisite.

questions.title: What makes this loader slow, and how do you fix it?

options.text: It's a needless **request waterfall**: `fetchConfig` doesn't depend on `user`, yet `await` makes it wait until `fetchUser` resolves â€” ~400 ms total. Start both first and `await Promise.all([...])` to run them concurrently (~200 ms).
Nothing is wrong â€” sequential `await`s already run concurrently in JavaScript.
Wrap each call in `useMemo` so the results are cached between renders.
Add a `staleTime` so the second request is served from cache.

questions.title: Which techniques genuinely help eliminate request waterfalls?

options.text: Kick off independent requests together and `await Promise.all([...])` instead of awaiting them one after another.
Hoist fetching to the route/loader so it starts before components render (render-as-you-fetch), rather than each component fetching only after it mounts (fetch-on-render).
Use `useQueries` to fire a dynamic list of queries in parallel instead of relying on nested components that each fetch.
Raise `staleTime` â€” TanStack Query then runs independent first-load requests in parallel automatically.

questions.explanation:
Waterfalls come from **serialized starts** â€” a request that can't *begin* until something earlier finishes. The fixes all attack the *start time*:

- **`Promise.all`** overlaps requests that have no data dependency.
- **Render-as-you-fetch** moves the fetch *out* of the component tree (into a route loader or an event handler) so it begins immediately, instead of the classic component waterfall where a parent renders â†’ child mounts â†’ child fetches â†’ grandchild mounts â†’ fetchesâ€¦
- **`useQueries`** runs N queries concurrently from a single hook call, avoiding a chain of nested fetching components.

`staleTime` is the odd one out: it tunes *freshness* (whether an already-cached query refetches), not *parallelism*. On a cold first load there's nothing cached, so raising `staleTime` changes nothing about how the independent requests are scheduled.

questions.title: What does this `prefetchQuery` on hover accomplish?

options.text: It fetches the post and stores it in the cache ahead of navigation, so when the detail page's `useQuery(['post', id])` mounts it finds the data already cached and renders without a loading spinner.
It pre-renders the detail React component in a hidden offscreen tree, ready to swap in.
It opens a subscription that streams live updates for that post until you navigate away.
It does nothing unless the post is already in the cache; prefetch can only refresh existing entries.

questions.explanation:
`prefetchQuery` runs the same fetch a `useQuery` would, but **imperatively and ahead of time**, writing the result into the cache and returning a promise that resolves to nothing (you don't read its value directly). The payoff is latency hiding: the few hundred milliseconds a user spends moving toward and clicking a link are spent fetching, so the destination paints instantly from cache.

It does **not** render any component (that's React's job once you navigate), and it doesn't open a stream. It also works on a *cold* cache â€” that's the whole point of warming an entry that doesn't exist yet. One nuance worth knowing: `prefetchQuery` respects `staleTime`, so if a fresh copy is already cached it skips the network and returns immediately, making repeated hovers cheap.

questions.title: What's the idiomatic fix for refetching data that never changes?
questions.description: A `useQuery(['currencies'], fetchCurrencies)` loads a list of currency codes that **never** change during a session. With default options, navigating back to the page fires the request *every time*.

options.text: Set a high `staleTime` (e.g. `staleTime: Infinity`) so the query is treated as fresh and no background refetch fires on mount, focus, or reconnect.
Increase `gcTime` so the cached entry survives longer between visits.
Wrap `fetchCurrencies` in `useCallback` so its identity is stable across renders.
Set `networkMode: 'offlineFirst'` so the network is skipped when data exists.

questions.explanation:
The refetch happens because the **default `staleTime` is `0`**: data is stale the instant it arrives, so each re-mount (and each window focus / reconnect) sees a stale query and revalidates it in the background. For data that's effectively immutable within a session, declare it fresh forever with `staleTime: Infinity` â€” TanStack Query then serves the cache and never auto-refetches it.

Why the distractors fail:

- **`gcTime`** governs how long an *inactive* (unused) query stays in memory before garbage collection. The entry was present here â€” it was *stale*, not evicted â€” so a longer `gcTime` changes nothing about the refetch.
- **`useCallback`** stabilizes the function reference but has no effect on the cache's freshness policy; the query key, not the `queryFn` identity, drives refetching.
- **`networkMode`** controls behavior around connectivity (e.g. pausing when offline), not whether fresh-enough cached data is reused.

questions.title: How does switching to `useSuspenseQuery` change the component's design?
questions.description: You switch a component from `useQuery` to `useSuspenseQuery(['post', id], fetchPost)`.

options.text: `data` is guaranteed defined inside the component â€” there's no `isPending`/`undefined` branch to handle. The nearest `<Suspense>` boundary shows the fallback while loading, and an `<ErrorBoundary>` catches fetch errors.
`data` can still be `undefined` on the first render, so you keep the same loading guard you had with `useQuery`.
It disables caching for that query and refetches on every render.
It only works inside React Server Components and can't run on the client.

questions.explanation:
Suspense **inverts where loading and error states live**. `useSuspenseQuery` suspends rendering until data exists, so by the time your component body runs, `data` is non-nullable â€” TypeScript types it as `TData`, not `TData | undefined`, and the `if (isPending) return <Spinner/>` ladder disappears. Loading UI moves *up* to the nearest `<Suspense fallback={...}>`, and thrown fetch errors are caught by an `<ErrorBoundary>`:

```tsx
<ErrorBoundary fallback={<Failed />}>
  <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
    <Post id={id} /> {/* uses useSuspenseQuery; renders only with data */}
  </Suspense>
</ErrorBoundary>
```

The caching layer is unchanged â€” same query keys, same `staleTime`, same dedup; only the *consumption API* differs, so it absolutely runs on the client and needs no RSC. One sharp edge to remember: two `useSuspenseQuery` calls in the *same* component run sequentially (the first suspends before the second starts), recreating a waterfall â€” use `useSuspenseQueries` to fetch them in parallel.

questions.title: Which `useMutation` lifecycle callback runs *before* the mutation function?
questions.description: That's where you cancel in-flight queries, snapshot the current cache value, apply the optimistic update, and return a context object for rollback.
questions.explanation: It's **`onMutate`**. It fires synchronously before `mutationFn`, receives the same `variables` you passed to `mutate(...)`, and its return value is handed back to you as `context` in `onError` and `onSettled` â€” which is how the rollback snapshot survives long enough to be restored if the request fails. The full ordering is `onMutate` â†’ `mutationFn` â†’ (`onError` | `onSuccess`) â†’ `onSettled`. Because the optimistic write and its rollback snapshot both originate in `onMutate`, it's the heart of the optimistic-update recipe.
